text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, utilizing minimally invasive techniques to remove the gallbladder.
The surgeon places classical laparoscopic ports: one in the epigastrium, a tandem port in the hypogastrium, and a third port in the lumbar region, preparing for the cholecystectomy procedure.
The laparoscopic camera is positioned through the umbilical port, providing a view of the surgical field for the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the lumbar port to retract the gallbladder upwards, providing better exposure for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.
Upon inspecting the gallbladder area, an abnormal bulge is visible, indicating potential anatomical irregularities or pathology.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneum over the abnormal swelling in the gallbladder area, using instruments inserted through the lumbar port.
The surgeon observes a cord-like structure running down in the surgical field, likely in the vicinity of the gallbladder area.
The surgeon removes the peritoneum above the gallbladder, revealing a slow outpouching as the dissection continues.
The surgeon observes Hartmann's pouch, noting its thin walls. The cystic artery appears as a taut structure, abnormally positioned towards the right and anterior to the cystic duct.
The surgeon completes the dissection of Hartmann's pouch, revealing its abnormal appearance. The cystic artery is now being dissected, visible as a taut structure running anterior to the cystic duct.
The surgeon exposes a long cystic duct, clearly visualizing the junction between the cystic duct and the common bile duct (CBD).
The T junction between the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD) is clearly visible, reducing the risk of CBD injury during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon observes the cystic artery, which is abnormally positioned towards the right side of the cystic duct.
The surgeon creates a window between the cystic artery and the cystic duct, avoiding the posterior window approach.
The surgeon confirms that the cystic artery is visually observed entering the body of the gallbladder during the dissection.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery using LT clips, with the artery visibly positioned near the cystic duct.
The surgeon visually confirms the cystic artery entering the gallbladder, highlighting its anatomical path.
The surgeon carefully clips the cystic artery, which is abnormally positioned towards the right side of the cystic duct, ensuring precise placement of the clips.
The surgeon places two clips on each side of the cystic artery and cystic duct, securing these structures during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the cystic artery, enhancing the mobility of the gallbladder. The previously unclear pouch is now identified as part of the gallbladder.
The surgeon pulls down the gallbladder, creating an abnormal appearance as the cystic artery is manipulated.
The surgeon creates a classical posterior window by carefully dissecting the tissue behind the gallbladder, revealing the anatomical structures and enhancing visibility for further dissection.
After creating the posterior window, the surgeon begins dissecting the cystic duct, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides unnamed vessels surrounding the cystic duct while attempting to locate the junction between the infundibulum and the cystic duct.
The surgeon observes the abnormal outpouching of Hartman's pouch, which has descended and is now clearly visible as part of the gallbladder.
The surgeon continues the dissection by proceeding with the creation of the posterior window around the cystic duct, further exposing the anatomical structures for clearer visualization.

The surgeon has created a wide posterior window, ensuring the critical view of safety with no linear structures other than the cystic duct and artery visible near the gallbladder. The cystic duct is then ligated and divided.

The surgeon observes an abnormal arterial system near the cystic duct, identifying an additional vessel running parallel to the duct. The cystic duct has been completely skeletonized, exposing its structure for further surgical planning.
The surgeon divides the cystic duct between clips, following the confirmation of the critical view of safety and clear anatomical visualization.
The surgeon applies a Tenomum clip above the T junction, clearly visible at the junction of the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon observes the cystic duct, noting its sufficient length for further surgical manipulation.
In the laparoscopic view, the surgeon places clips on the distal side at the junction of the cystic duct and the gallbladder, completing the clipping process on the proximal side.
The surgeon divides the cystic duct between pairs of clips, facilitating the removal of the gallbladder from its bed.
The surgeon identifies the mesogallbladder, a peritoneal fold connecting the gallbladder bed to the gallbladder, preparing to separate these structures.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect the mesogoldbladder, which is visibly long, ensuring it is not an intrahepatic gallbladder. The dissection progresses slowly.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, completing the final step of the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully divides the peritoneal attachment of the gallbladder to the liver, progressively detaching the gallbladder from its bed.
The surgeon continues the dissection, approaching the final step of detaching the gallbladder from the liver bed, using a harmonic scalpel to separate the mesogallbladder or peritoneal fold.
The surgeon approaches the gallbladder fundus, attempting to remove it in a reverse manner after completing the dissection lower down.
The surgeon connects the dissected tissue planes to complete the separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed, finalizing the dissection process.

The surgeon divides the cystic artery, causing the taut portion of the Hartmann's pouch to disappear, revealing the cystic dilatation. The dissection follows basic principles, including creating a white posterior window to fully separate the anatomical structures.
The surgeon divides the cystic duct, ensuring there are no visible ductal abnormalities during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon inspects the gallbladder area and confirms the absence of a cholecystohepatic duct, known as the duct of Lushka.
The surgeon begins the dissection of Calot's triangle, focusing on carefully separating the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder, ensuring all anatomical structures are clearly separated and visible.
The gallbladder is fully detached from the liver bed, marking the completion of the cholecystectomy procedure.
The gallbladder is extracted through the epigastric tenenum port using a clofosub's instrument.
The surgeon holds the cystic duct end and carefully extracts it through the tenenum port.
The surgeon gently manipulates and removes the gallbladder through the port after completing the dissection.
